steps.title: Identify the key pattern showing the researchers' concern
steps.content:
The graph shows total seagrass coverage rising dramatically from ~18,000 hectares in 2012 to ~44,000 hectares by late 2018—driven by widgeon grass increasing from ~6,000 to ~18,000 hectares—while eelgrass remains flat.

Then, in early 2019, all three lines drop sharply: eelgrass to ~3,000, widgeon grass to ~9,000, and total coverage to ~27,000.

The researchers' caution is illustrated by this pattern: total coverage increased substantially, but when an environmental shock struck in early 2019, the entire system collapsed together.

steps.title: Match the 2019 collapse to the correct explanation
steps.content:
Choice A accounts for this data: algal blooms in early 2019 block sunlight, preventing photosynthesis in all seagrass species simultaneously.

This matches the sharp, synchronized decline across eelgrass, widgeon grass, and total coverage in 2019.

Choice B describes a fungal infection in 2018, but the graph shows no decline in 2018; widgeon grass continues rising through late 2018, ruling out a 2018 infection.

Choice C explains the 2012–2017 increase but ignores the 2019 collapse.

Choice D suggests that only widgeon grass can tolerate the 2019 temperature spike, yet the graph shows widgeon grass coverage dropping in 2019, not thriving.

Choice A directly illustrates the researchers' point: despite gaining 26,000 hectares of coverage, the ecosystem became vulnerable to a different environmental shock (algal blooms blocking light), demonstrating that total coverage did not translate to resilience. That matches choice A — A is correct.
